title: Inherent flux crosstalk and coupler-driven single-qubit gates in superconducting circuits
abstract: Crosstalk refers to unwanted qubit addressing. This is particularly detrimental when scaling up quantum information systems because unintended interactions limit their overall performance. For superconducting qubits, tunable couplings and frequency tunability achieved through externally applied magnetic fluxes enable high-fidelity entangling gates; however, they also introduce crosstalk through unintended flux coupling. In this work, we investigate the impact of time-dependent external magnetic fluxes in quantized circuits on superconducting qubit couplings. We find that non-trivial cross-voltage driving emerges between capacitively linked qubits when the magnetic flux threading the SQUID loop of a qubit varies in time, in a manner analogous to Faraday's law of induction. Crucially, we show that this effect enables fast single qubit control through the coupler element in standard tunable-coupler architectures, potentially eliminating the need for individual microwave $XY$ control lines.
summary: The paper theoretically analyzes inherent flux crosstalk in superconducting qubit circuits by deriving the system dynamics using Lagrangian mechanics. It shows this unintended coupling, arising from time-varying external fluxes, can be harnessed to drive fast single-qubit gates via the coupler element. This approach promises high fidelity quantum control while simplifying hardware requirements.
main_problem: The work investigates inherent flux crosstalk—an unavoidable coupling mechanism arising from time-varying external magnetic fluxes in superconducting qubits—and demonstrates how to harness it for fast, high-fidelity single-qubit gates.
main_result: By utilizing the inherent flux crosstalk generated by modulating the coupler element, researchers can achieve fast single-qubit gate fidelities exceeding 99.9%, potentially eliminating the need for dedicated microwave control lines per qubit.
method: The analysis employs Lagrangian mechanics derived from lumped-element circuit models to derive the system Hamiltonian, followed by normal mode transformations and time-dependent perturbation theory for gate simulation.
model_or_system: The physical platform is a superconducting transmon qubit architecture featuring tunable couplers. The model uses generalized coordinates ($\Phi$) and derives the dynamics via a Lagrangian/Hamiltonian formalism that explicitly includes terms arising from $\dot{\Phi}_{	ext{ext}}(t)$.
key_observables: Average Gate Fidelity (AGF), Total Leakage Rate, Pauli error probabilities ($p_a$), and the effective drive strength ($\Omega_p$).
important_parameters: External flux modulation amplitude ($A$), static bias fluxes ($\phi_{st}$), and gate time ($t_g \sim 20 	ext{ ns}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the validity of the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA) and truncating the Hamiltonian expansion to include only first and second harmonic terms.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the circuit model, the derivation of the Lagrangian/Hamiltonian, and crucially, show AGF vs. gate time curves demonstrating high fidelity achievable when inherent crosstalk is included in the drive calculation.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from deriving the fundamental theory using Lagrangian mechanics to identify the inherent flux crosstalk term; it then applies this understanding by modeling the system's Hamiltonian in normal modes and finally simulates single-qubit gates, demonstrating performance metrics like AGF.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete theoretical framework for engineering quantum control pulses. For AMO/open systems theorists, the derivation of an unavoidable coupling term (inherent crosstalk) that can be actively exploited is valuable, as it moves beyond treating crosstalk purely as noise to treat it as a controllable resource.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The lumped element model of a superconducting transmon qubit. In panel a, the illustration of the circuit of the full qubit stack inside a dilution refrigerator, with a ded- icated coplanar waveguide readout resonator, XY microwave control line, and Z flux bias line. The shaded rectangle illus- trates a transmon qubit, formed by a SQUID loop shunted by a cross-shaped capacitance. In panel b, the shaded re- gion is modeled as an idealized lumped circuit with a control- lable external magnetic flux threading the SQUID loop of the transmon, as well as showcasing the tree (red) and the chord (black) branches of the circuit graph.
Figure 2. The lumped-element circuit of capacitively coupled tunable transmons showcasing the chosen tree (red) and the chord (black) branches of the network graph.

Figure 3. The lumped-element circuit model of the tunable coupler architecture. The red branches correspond to the cho- sen tree of the circuit graph, with capacitances CL,j. The chords (black) are the coupling capacitances C1c and C2c, branches of the Josepshson junctions EJ,j (left), vjEJ,j (right) and capacitances (right) CR,j. In these, j = 1, c, 2 and the labels are omitted for visual clarity.

Figure 4. The strengths of the linear drive terms Ω1 (blue) and Ω3 (red) as a function of the modulation amplitude A. For comparison, we also depict these with neglected inherent crosstalk (βj = 0, dashed-line), which clearly demonstrates the significance of Eq. (30). The circuit parameters used in this plot are CΣ,1 = 77 fF, CΣ,c = 56 fF, CΣ,2 = 75 fF, C1c = 2.5 fF, Cc2 = 2.5 fF, EJ,1/2π = 10 GHz, v1 = 1, EJ,c/2π = 12 GHz, vc = 0.95, EJ,2/2π = 10.2 GHz, v2 = 1, φ(1) st /2π = 0.075, φ(c) st /2π = 0.45, φ(2) st /2π = 0. We furthermore assumed CR,j = CL,j for all j = 1, c, 2.

Figure 5. The average gate fidelities (AGF) and total leakage rates (TL) as a function of the gate time for a coupler-driven X gate with rectangular pulse envelope. On panel a, the static flux is set to φ(1) st /π = 0.15 which results in a detuning for qubit 2 that is close to its anharmonicity. This leads to large leakage probability and limits the time of the gate. On panel b, the static flux is reduced to φ(1) st /π = 0.09, which increases ∆3 + α3 and leads to better AGF for shorter gate times. Panel c shows the contributions of the coupler, qubit 1 and qubit 2 modes to the total leakage, respectively. The periodicity of these provides fingerprints for the identification of leakage...
